---
title: "Theme Test"
subtitle: "Brand & Quarto Feature Showcase"
author:
  - name:
      given: Mohamed
      family: Bakr
    url: https://portfolio.mohdbakr.com
date: today
date-modified: today
abstract: |
  A comprehensive test page for verifying the site brand theme.
  Covers standard Quarto features: figures, tables, mathematics, callouts, code blocks,
  tabsets, mermaid diagrams, typography, layout, footnotes, and citations.
keywords:
  - Quarto
  - Theme
  - Brand
lang: en
jupyter: python3
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
copyright: "© 2026 Mohamed Bakr"
references:
  - id: example_2024
    author:
      - family: Example
        given: Jane
      - family: Author
        given: John
    container-title: Journal of Examples
    doi: 10.1234/example.2024
    issued: 2024
    title: "An Example Article for Demonstrating Citations"
  - id: canouil_2026
    author:
      - family: Canouil
        given: Mickaël
    issued: 2026
    title: "Branded Figures and Tables in R and Python with Quarto"
    url: https://mickael.canouil.fr/posts/2026-04-15-quarto-brand-figures-tables/
---

{{< include /_brand_setup.qmd >}}

## Introduction {#sec-intro}

This page tests every standard Quarto feature against the site brand theme.
Use the table of contents to jump to a specific section.

### Document Organisation

1. **Quarto Features** (@sec-quarto): Figures, tables, mathematics, callouts, tabsets, and code blocks.
2. **Typography and Layout** (@sec-typography): Headings, lists, quotations, and multi-column layout.
3. **Advanced Features** (@sec-advanced): Footnotes, citations, and cross-references.

## Quarto Features {#sec-quarto}

### Figures {#sec-figures}

#### Simple Figure

```{python}
#| label: fig-example
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "An example figure demonstrating basic figure capabilities with caption and cross-referencing."
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
apply_brand_theme()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.add_patch(
    plt.Rectangle(
        (0, 0),
        1,
        1,
        transform=ax.transAxes,
        facecolor=c["hero"],
        alpha=0.12,
        edgecolor="none",
    )
)
ax.text(
    0.5,
    0.55,
    "Figure Example",
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=22,
    fontweight="bold",
    color=c["hero"],
)
ax.text(
    0.5,
    0.40,
    f"brand primary: {c['hero']}",
    transform=ax.transAxes,
    ha="center",
    va="center",
    fontsize=11,
    color=c["sub"],
)
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Reference the figure using @fig-example in your text.

#### Subfigures

```{python}
#| label: fig-multiple
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "Two subfigures arranged side by side demonstrating multi-panel layouts."
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "First subfigure showing primary colour panel."
#|   - "Second subfigure showing annotation colour panel."
#| layout-ncol: 2
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
apply_brand_theme()

for label, color, tag in [
    ("Panel A", c["hero"], "primary"),
    ("Panel B", c["annot"], "secondary"),
]:
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4, 3))
    ax.add_patch(
        plt.Rectangle(
            (0, 0),
            1,
            1,
            transform=ax.transAxes,
            facecolor=color,
            alpha=0.15,
            edgecolor="none",
        )
    )
    ax.text(
        0.5,
        0.55,
        label,
        transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="center",
        va="center",
        fontsize=18,
        fontweight="bold",
        color=color,
    )
    ax.text(
        0.5,
        0.40,
        f"brand {tag}: {color}",
        transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="center",
        va="center",
        fontsize=10,
        color=c["sub"],
    )
    ax.set_xticks([])
    ax.set_yticks([])
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```

### Tables {#sec-tables}

#### Simple Table

| Column A | Column B | Column C |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Value 1  | Value 2  | Value 3  |
| Value 4  | Value 5  | Value 6  |
| Value 7  | Value 8  | Value 9  |

: A simple table demonstrating basic table capabilities. {#tbl-simple}

#### Brand Colour Reference

| Feature         | Light Mode  | Dark Mode   | Notes                           |
|-----------------|:-----------:|:-----------:|---------------------------------|
| Background      | `#F9FAFB`   | `#111827`   | Page background colour          |
| Foreground      | `#0B1F3A`   | `#F9FAFB`   | Primary text colour             |
| Accent          | `#14B8A6`   | `#14B8A6`   | Teal brand colour               |
| Emphasis        | `#C5A46D`   | `#C5A46D`   | Gold — sparingly only           |

: Mohamed Bakr brand colour scheme between light and dark modes. {#tbl-colours}

### Mathematics {#sec-maths}

#### Inline Mathematics

Inline expressions like $\alpha + \beta = \gamma$,
$\int_0^1 f(x) \, dx$,
and $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ integrate seamlessly with text.

#### Display Mathematics

Einstein's mass-energy equivalence:

$$
E = mc^2
$$ {#eq-einstein}

The quadratic formula:

$$
x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}
$$ {#eq-quadratic}

Bayes' theorem:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A)\, P(A)}{P(B)}
$$ {#eq-bayes}

### Callouts {#sec-callouts}

::: {.callout-note}
Note callouts provide additional information or context without interrupting the main narrative flow.
:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Pro Tip

Tip callouts offer helpful suggestions, best practices, or recommendations.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Important Information

Important callouts highlight essential details that readers must not miss.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Warning

Warning callouts alert readers to caveats, limitations, or potential issues.
:::

::: {.callout-caution}
## Caution

Caution callouts warn about significant risks, dangers, or problematic scenarios.
:::

### Panel Tabsets {#sec-tabsets}

::: {.panel-tabset}

#### Overview

This tab contains introductory content.

#### Python

```python
# Bayes theorem example
def bayes(p_a, p_b_given_a, p_b):
    return (p_b_given_a * p_a) / p_b

posterior = bayes(0.01, 0.9, 0.11)
print(f"P(A|B) = {posterior:.4f}")
```

#### R

```r
# Bayes theorem in R
bayes <- function(p_a, p_b_given_a, p_b) {
  (p_b_given_a * p_a) / p_b
}

posterior <- bayes(0.01, 0.9, 0.11)
cat(sprintf("P(A|B) = %.4f\n", posterior))
```

:::

### Code Blocks {#sec-code}

```{.python filename="probability.py"}
from scipy.stats import norm
import numpy as np

def confidence_interval(data, confidence=0.95):
    """Compute confidence interval for a sample mean."""
    n = len(data)
    mean = np.mean(data)
    se = norm.ppf((1 + confidence) / 2) * (np.std(data, ddof=1) / np.sqrt(n))
    return mean - se, mean + se
```

```{.r filename="probability.R"}
confidence_interval <- function(data, confidence = 0.95) {
  n <- length(data)
  mean_val <- mean(data)
  se <- qnorm((1 + confidence) / 2) * (sd(data) / sqrt(n))
  c(mean_val - se, mean_val + se)
}
```

### Mermaid Diagrams {#sec-mermaid}

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: "A simple flowchart demonstrating Mermaid diagram support."
%%| label: fig-mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start] --> B{Decision}
    B -->|Yes| C[Action A]
    B -->|No| D[Action B]
    C --> E[End]
    D --> E
```

See @fig-mermaid for the rendered diagram.

### Code Annotations {#sec-annotations}

```python
def calculate_area(radius: float) -> float: # <1>
    """Calculate the area of a circle.""" # <2>
    import math # <3>
    return math.pi * radius ** 2 # <4>
```

1. Function signature with type hints for input and output.
2. Docstring describing the function purpose.
3. Import the `math` module for access to `pi`.
4. Apply the area formula: $A = \pi r^2$.

### Data Visualisation {#sec-viz}

Four *Storytelling with Data* principles applied throughout:

- **Title = insight**: headline states the finding, not just the topic
- **Purposeful colour**: one hero hue; grey for everything else
- **Minimal ink**: every gridline, spine, and tick must earn its place
- **Direct labels**: text lives on the chart — no floating legend boxes

Charts use transparent backgrounds and re-render per theme via `renderings: [light, dark]`. Colours are read at render time from `_brand.yml` via the `QUARTO_EXECUTE_INFO` environment variable (@canouil_2026). Derived tones (muted text, gridlines) are computed with `_col_mix()` so nothing is hardcoded — switching `_brand.yml` updates every output automatically.

Tables use `great_tables` with a `gt_brand()` helper that applies brand colours and fonts. Because tables cannot use `renderings`, light and dark variants are wrapped in `.light-content` / `.dark-content` divs so the correct version shows for each theme.

#### Learning Curve

```{python}
#| label: fig-learning-curve
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "Two cohorts of 60 students tracked over 8 weeks on conditional probability assessments."
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
apply_brand_theme()

weeks  = np.arange(0, 9)
daily  = np.array([0, 12, 28, 45, 63, 79, 89, 95, 98])
weekly = np.array([0,  7, 13, 20, 31, 41, 53, 63, 71])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))

ax.plot(weeks, weekly, color=c["muted"], linewidth=2.5, solid_capstyle="round", zorder=2)
ax.plot(weeks, daily,  color=c["hero"],  linewidth=2.5, solid_capstyle="round", zorder=3)
ax.fill_between(weeks, weekly, daily, alpha=0.07, color=c["hero"])

ax.text(8.2, 98, "Daily\npractice", color=c["hero"],  va="center",
        fontsize=9, fontweight="bold", linespacing=1.4)
ax.text(8.2, 71, "Weekly\nreview",  color=c["muted"], va="center",
        fontsize=9, linespacing=1.4)

ax.annotate(
    "Gap opens\nat week 4",
    xy=(4, 63), xytext=(1.8, 83),
    fontsize=9, color=c["annot"],
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=c["annot"], lw=1.2,
                    connectionstyle="arc3,rad=-0.3"),
)

for y in [25, 50, 75, 100]:
    ax.axhline(y, color=c["grid"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=0)

ax.set_xlim(-0.3, 11.5)
ax.set_ylim(0, 112)
ax.set_xticks(weeks)
ax.set_xticklabels(["Start"] + [f"Wk {w}" for w in weeks[1:]], fontsize=9)
ax.set_yticks([0, 25, 50, 75, 100])
ax.set_yticklabels(["0 %", "25 %", "50 %", "75 %", "100 %"], fontsize=9)
ax.tick_params(length=0)
ax.set_title(
    "Daily practice produces 38 % higher mastery after eight weeks",
    loc="left", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=10,
)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0.88, 1])
plt.show()
```

#### Common Misconceptions

```{python}
#| label: fig-misconceptions
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "Survey of 200 data science students on which probability concepts cause the most persistent confusion."
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
apply_brand_theme()

concepts = [
    "Gambler's Fallacy",
    "Joint vs. conditional probability",
    "p-value interpretation",
    "Bayes' theorem in practice",
    "Law of Large Numbers",
    "Independence vs. correlation",
]
pct    = [82, 71, 68, 54, 41, 35]
colors = [c["hero"]] + [c["muted"]] * (len(pct) - 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))

bars = ax.barh(range(len(concepts)), pct, color=colors, height=0.55)

for bar, val, col in zip(bars, pct, colors):
    weight = "bold" if val == max(pct) else "normal"
    ax.text(
        bar.get_width() + 1.5,
        bar.get_y() + bar.get_height() / 2,
        f"{val} %",
        va="center", fontsize=10, color=col, fontweight=weight,
    )

ax.set_yticks(range(len(concepts)))
ax.set_yticklabels(concepts, fontsize=10)
ax.invert_yaxis()

for spine in ax.spines.values():
    spine.set_visible(False)
ax.xaxis.set_visible(False)
ax.tick_params(left=False, bottom=False)
ax.set_xlim(0, 110)
ax.set_title(
    "The Gambler's Fallacy is the most persistent probability misconception",
    loc="left", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=10,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

#### Bayesian Updating — Interactive

```{python}
#| label: fig-bayesian-update
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "How prior beliefs shift toward the true rate as evidence accumulates."
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
x = np.linspace(0, 1, 300)

def _trace(a, b, color, name):
    return go.Scatter(x=x, y=beta_dist.pdf(x, a, b), mode="lines", name=name,
                      line=dict(color=color, width=2.5))

fig = go.Figure([
    _trace(2,    8,    c["muted"], "Prior  Beta(2, 8)"),
    _trace(2+5,  8+5,  c["annot"], "10 obs  Beta(7, 13)"),
    _trace(2+20, 8+20, c["hero"],  "40 obs  Beta(22, 28)"),
])
fig.add_vline(x=0.44, line_dash="dot", line_color=c["sub"],
              annotation_text="true rate 0.44", annotation_position="top right",
              annotation_font_color=c["sub"])
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text="More evidence pulls beliefs toward the true rate",
               font=dict(size=14, color=c["text"]), x=0, xanchor="left"),
    xaxis=dict(title="Estimated success rate", tickformat=".0%",
               showgrid=True, gridcolor=c["grid"], zeroline=False, color=c["sub"]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Probability density", showgrid=False, zeroline=False,
               color=c["sub"]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)", font=dict(color=c["text"])),
    plot_bgcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    paper_bgcolor="rgba(0,0,0,0)",
    font=dict(color=c["text"]),
    margin=dict(t=60, b=40, l=60, r=20),
    hovermode="x unified",
)
fig.show()
```

*Drag to explore how a sceptical prior (Beta(2,8)) narrows toward the true success rate as observations accumulate.*

#### Probability Distributions — Branded Table {#sec-branded-table}

Tables styled with `gt_brand()` inherit the active brand's colours, fonts, and
palette automatically.  Because `great_tables` does not support the
`renderings` cell option, light and dark variants are wrapped in
`.light-content` / `.dark-content` divs (@canouil_2026).

```{python}
#| label: setup-table-data
#| include: false

dist_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Distribution": ["Normal", "Beta", "Exponential", "Poisson", "Binomial"],
    "Notation":     ["N(μ, σ²)", "Beta(α, β)", "Exp(λ)", "Pois(λ)", "Bin(n, p)"],
    "Mean":         ["μ", "α / (α + β)", "1 / λ", "λ", "np"],
    "Variance":     ["σ²", "αβ / ((α+β)²(α+β+1))", "1 / λ²", "λ", "np(1 − p)"],
    "Support":      ["(−∞, ∞)", "[0, 1]", "[0, ∞)", "{0, 1, 2, …}", "{0, 1, …, n}"],
})
```

::: {.light-content}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-distributions-light
#| tbl-cap: "Common probability distributions and their key properties (light mode)."
gt_brand(dist_data, brand_mode="light")
```

:::

::: {.dark-content}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-distributions-dark
#| tbl-cap: "Common probability distributions and their key properties (dark mode)."
gt_brand(dist_data, brand_mode="dark")
```

:::

#### Sample‑Size vs. Power — Area Chart {#sec-power-chart}

```{python}
#| label: fig-power-curve
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "Statistical power rises steeply for small sample sizes, then plateaus — diminishing returns beyond n ≈ 120."
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
apply_brand_theme()

n = np.arange(10, 201)
effect_sizes = {"Large (d = 0.8)": 0.8, "Medium (d = 0.5)": 0.5, "Small (d = 0.2)": 0.2}
from scipy.stats import norm as sp_norm

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))

palette_colors = [c["hero"], c["annot"], c["muted"]]

for idx, (label, d) in enumerate(effect_sizes.items()):
    se = 1 / np.sqrt(n)
    z_crit = sp_norm.ppf(0.975)
    power = 1 - sp_norm.cdf(z_crit - d / se)
    color = palette_colors[idx % len(palette_colors)]
    ax.plot(n, power * 100, color=color, linewidth=2.5, solid_capstyle="round")
    ax.fill_between(n, 0, power * 100, alpha=0.05, color=color)
    y_end = power[-1] * 100
    ax.text(203, y_end, label, color=color, va="center", fontsize=9,
            fontweight="bold" if idx == 0 else "normal")

ax.axhline(80, color=c["grid"], linewidth=1.2, linestyle="--", zorder=0)
ax.text(12, 82, "80 % convention", fontsize=8, color=c["sub"])

for y in [25, 50, 75, 100]:
    ax.axhline(y, color=c["grid"], linewidth=0.5, zorder=0)

ax.set_xlim(10, 230)
ax.set_ylim(0, 105)
ax.set_xticks([10, 50, 100, 150, 200])
ax.set_yticks([0, 25, 50, 75, 100])
ax.set_yticklabels(["0 %", "25 %", "50 %", "75 %", "100 %"], fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel("Sample size (n)", fontsize=10)
ax.tick_params(length=0)
ax.set_title(
    "Larger effect sizes reach 80 % power with far fewer observations",
    loc="left", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=10,
)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 0.82, 1])
plt.show()
```

#### Correlation Scatter — Palette Colours {#sec-scatter}

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter-groups
#| renderings: [light, dark]
#| fig-cap: "Simulated study-hours vs. exam-score data for three teaching methods, coloured with the brand palette."
#| code-fold: true

c = get_chart_colors()
apply_brand_theme()

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)

groups = {
    "Active recall": (6.0, 75, 1.8, 8),
    "Spaced repetition": (7.5, 82, 1.5, 7),
    "Passive review": (5.5, 60, 2.0, 10),
}
palette_colors = [c["hero"], c["annot"], c["muted"]]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))

for idx, (name, (x_mu, y_mu, x_sd, y_sd)) in enumerate(groups.items()):
    n_pts = 40
    x = rng.normal(x_mu, x_sd, n_pts)
    y = rng.normal(y_mu, y_sd, n_pts)
    color = palette_colors[idx % len(palette_colors)]
    ax.scatter(x, y, s=40, alpha=0.75, color=color, edgecolors="none", zorder=3)
    ax.text(x.mean() + 0.3, y.mean() + 1.5, name, fontsize=9, color=color,
            fontweight="bold", ha="center")

for y in [50, 60, 70, 80, 90]:
    ax.axhline(y, color=c["grid"], linewidth=0.5, zorder=0)

ax.set_xlabel("Study hours per week", fontsize=10)
ax.set_ylabel("Exam score", fontsize=10)
ax.tick_params(length=0)
ax.set_title(
    "Active-recall students score higher per hour of study",
    loc="left", fontsize=12, fontweight="bold", pad=10,
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

#### Assessment Metrics — Branded Table {#sec-metrics-table}

```{python}
#| label: setup-metrics-data
#| include: false

metrics_data = pd.DataFrame({
    "Method":    ["Active recall", "Spaced repetition", "Passive review"],
    "n":         [40, 40, 40],
    "Mean score": [75.2, 82.1, 60.4],
    "Std dev":   [8.0, 7.0, 10.0],
    "95 % CI":   ["72.7 – 77.7", "79.9 – 84.3", "57.3 – 63.5"],
    "p-value":   ["< 0.001", "< 0.001", "ref"],
})
```

::: {.light-content}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-metrics-light
#| tbl-cap: "Assessment metrics by teaching method (light mode)."
gt_brand(metrics_data, brand_mode="light")
```

:::

::: {.dark-content}

```{python}
#| label: tbl-metrics-dark
#| tbl-cap: "Assessment metrics by teaching method (dark mode)."
gt_brand(metrics_data, brand_mode="dark")
```

:::

## Typography and Layout {#sec-typography}

### Heading Levels {#sec-headings}

This section demonstrates heading hierarchy.

### Level 3 Heading

Content under level 3 heading demonstrating hierarchy and spacing.

#### Level 4 Heading

Content under level 4 heading with appropriate font sizing.

##### Level 5 Heading

Content under level 5 heading maintaining visual consistency.

### Lists {#sec-lists}

#### Unordered Lists

- First item demonstrating unordered list styling.
- Second item with nested elements:
  - Nested item 2.1 showing second-level indentation.
  - Nested item 2.2 with consistent formatting.
- Third item returning to top level.

#### Ordered Lists

1. First numbered item establishing sequence.
2. Second numbered item with sub-steps:
   a. Sub-step 2.1 using alphabetic numbering.
   b. Sub-step 2.2 maintaining hierarchy.
3. Third numbered item completing the sequence.

### Quotations {#sec-quotes}

> "The goal is to turn data into information, and information into insight."
>
> — Carly Fiorina

### Layout Features {#sec-layout}

#### Multi-Column Layout

::: {layout-ncol=2}
**Column 1:** Content in the first column demonstrating side-by-side layout capabilities.

**Column 2:** Content in the second column showing parallel information.
:::

## Advanced Features {#sec-advanced}

### Footnotes {#sec-footnotes}

This sentence includes a footnote.[^1]
Another sentence demonstrates multiple footnotes working together.[^2]

[^1]: First footnote providing additional context or explanatory information.

[^2]: Second footnote demonstrating how multiple footnotes coexist.

### Citations and Bibliography {#sec-citations}

Citations integrate seamlessly with the bibliography system.
See @example_2024 for a comprehensive example of citation formatting.

### Cross-References {#sec-refs}

**Sections:** @sec-intro, @sec-quarto, @sec-figures, @sec-tables, @sec-maths.

**Figures:** @fig-example, @fig-mermaid, @fig-multiple (specifically @fig-multiple-1 and @fig-multiple-2), @fig-power-curve, @fig-scatter-groups.

**Tables:** @tbl-simple, @tbl-colours, @tbl-distributions-light, @tbl-metrics-light.

**Equations:** @eq-einstein, @eq-quadratic, @eq-bayes.

## Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

This page exercises all standard Quarto features for visual verification of the brand theme.
Toggle between light and dark mode to confirm both variants render correctly.

### Brand Colours Applied

The table below reflects the **active** `_brand.yml` palette.
All charts and branded tables read these values at render time;
switching to a different brand file updates every output automatically.

| Role        | Semantic Key   | Description                     |
|-------------|:--------------:|---------------------------------|
| Background  | `background`   | Page background colour          |
| Foreground  | `foreground`   | Primary body text colour        |
| Primary     | `primary`      | Hero / link colour              |
| Secondary   | `secondary`    | Annotation / warning colour     |
| Tertiary    | `tertiary`     | Success / info accent           |

### Render

```bash
quarto render example.qmd
```

## References {.references}

::: {#refs}
:::
